title: Some Weird Realization
wikitext:
[]: Some Weird Realization             Quote Post by Tumbling Down » Wed Dec 17, 2014 11:13 pm This doesn't warrant its own topic.. but we have a chit-chat section, so I guess that's fine? So I was mentally ranking everything I've seen in the Evangelion franchise. Neon Genesis Evangelion: Okay. The End of Evangelion: ABSOLUTELY MAGNIFICENT! Evangelion 1.11: Pretty darn good! Evangelion 2.22: Pretty darn good! Evangelion: 3.33: What? Umm.. okay? Okay. The three things in this series that I really enjoyed have good dubs. The two that I didn't really enjoy either don't have a dub or have a very bad dub. Could it be that I'm more likely to enjoy something if it's dubbed? I do generally choose dubs because subs strain my eyes. (Which for some reason is considered a valid issue in 2D vs. 3D discussions but not dubs vs subs discussions.) That's very different from my ability to resonate with a work being affected, though. This is of interest to me because I like to try to figure out why I enjoy the media I enjoy, especially in cases where my feelings are inconsistent, such as with Eva. (Also of note is that I think I prefer the subbed version of EoE more than the dubbed version because having English dialogue distracts from Komm Susser Tod, which is a wonderful song. I've only seen EoE in full twice, though.)

[]:             Quote Post by IronEvangelion » Thu Dec 18, 2014 4:22 am I think your ability to enjoy a show depends on whether or not it has a dub, and how good the dub is. Straining your eyes is definitely going to adversely affect your ability to enjoy a form of visual media. I myself usually prefer subs because I think the Japanese voices fit the characters better (in addition to having a calming musical quality to them that I don't hear in English dubs). Sometimes, however, I find the English dub to sound so much better than the Japanese dub that I actually prefer the English dub. At first I couldn't stand the English dub of Nadia, but as I watched the series I got so used to it that I actually grew to like it, and now hearing the characters speaking Japanese just sounds wrong.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Dec 18, 2014 7:34 am The decision for subs is simple for me: I wan to see the works of this director, including his work directing the voice cast. Listening to someone else direct a difference voice cast isn’t enjoying the works of the filmmaker, it’s enjoying the works of others while trying to watch stuff from the filmmaker. If Anno or Miyazaki didn’t direct the voice cast, then I can’t blame any whininess in a character on the filmmaker (such as the Nostalgia Critic seems to be doing for his Studio Ghibli reviews). I find that akin to watching the Spanish dub for an American English film and blaming the American director and cast members on why the Spanish voice to a character didn’t sound right. Subs are undoubtably a more honest representation of the film you’re watching. Enjoyability is another discussion all together. If you enjoy something a certain way, then you should watch it that way. I’m not going to stand in anyone’s way for a dub, even if it means watching it myself just to hang out with a few people who wanted to watch the dub. If you watched the dub and didn’t enjoy the film, then try to separate the dub from the film in your head as best you can. (Re-watch it with subtitles if you have to.) Did the animation still hold up? How about the pacing of the film? Did the music and cinematography set the tone properly? How did the blocking of the on-screen characters, their spacial relation to each other, and their spacial relation to the camera’s lens and (lens type) help tell the story? If you watch the dub, then these are most likely the only surviving aspects of the film that properly convey the film’s tone and emotional intent. (Even an accurately translated English dub runs the risk of misrepresenting the tone execution from the original cast, and shouldn’t really be considered beyond plot revelations and such.)

[]:             Quote Post by The Cruel » Thu Dec 18, 2014 12:38 pm I've seen NGE & EoE 8x Times and NTE 6x Times. I watched them mostly in german but also in Japanese and english. All dubs are great and original on it's, own especially if one dub has something the others doesn't have. As a example: In the Japanese and english dub of 2.0, Asuka screams inside the Entry-Plug of Unit-03 when Unit 01, being controled by the Dummy-Plug, bit it in halves. In the german version of 2.0 she doesn't scream, wich makes it seem that she was unconcious the whole time. And because that 3.0+1.0 'll be only available in Japanese and subbed at first, then 'll watch it right away but 'll be also ready for the other dubs when they come out.

[]:             Quote Post by EvangelionGodMode » Thu Dec 18, 2014 2:31 pm Its all a matter of opinion whether it really bothers you to listen to the show in a different language from the one it originally comes from or not. Me personally ive never let such a minor thing get in my way of the viewing experience and personally i find the dub not to be that bad in NGE and really good in the rest of the Eva. The only dub ive ever really found to be really bad was the Mirai Nikki

[]:             Quote Post by Tumbling Down » Thu Dec 18, 2014 3:31 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: The decision for subs is simple for me: I wan to see the works of this director, including his work directing the voice cast. Listening to someone else direct a difference voice cast isn’t enjoying the works of the filmmaker, it’s enjoying the works of others while trying to watch stuff from the filmmaker. If Anno or Miyazaki didn’t direct the voice cast, then I can’t blame any whininess in a character on the filmmaker (such as the Nostalgia Critic seems to be doing for his Studio Ghibli reviews). I find that akin to watching the Spanish dub for an American English film and blaming the American director and cast members on why the Spanish voice to a character didn’t sound right. Subs are undoubtably a more honest representation of the film you’re watching. Enjoyability is another discussion all together. If you enjoy something a certain way, then you should watch it that way. I’m not going to stand in anyone’s way for a dub, even if it means watching it myself just to hang out with a few people who wanted to watch the dub. If you watched the dub and didn’t enjoy the film, then try to separate the dub from the film in your head as best you can. (Re-watch it with subtitles if you have to.) Did the animation still hold up? How about the pacing of the film? Did the music and cinematography set the tone properly? How did the blocking of the on-screen characters, their spacial relation to each other, and their spacial relation to the camera’s lens and (lens type) help tell the story? If you watch the dub, then these are most likely the only surviving aspects of the film that properly convey the film’s tone and emotional intent. (Even an accurately translated English dub runs the risk of misrepresenting the tone execution from the original cast, and shouldn’t really be considered beyond plot revelations and such.)

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Thu Dec 18, 2014 11:06 pm Tumbling Down wrote: I can't look at what's happening, I'm busy reading the text.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Dec 18, 2014 11:09 pm That is very much not the norm in the US, and I find them distracting for the same reason. Unless the dub is simply awful I always go that route.

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Thu Dec 18, 2014 11:31 pm ^ If you enjoy it that way, then I don't have a problem with it. But when someone complains about the subs harming the film itself (in this case the surreal imagery), I find it irritating because watching films with subs is just a matter of getting used to it and people who aren't used to it complain about it too much without even trying properly.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Dec 18, 2014 11:59 pm If I'm reading the dialog I can't read the visuals.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Fri Dec 19, 2014 1:49 am Oz wrote: I find it irritating because watching films with subs is just a matter of getting used to it and people who aren't used to it complain about it too much without even trying properly.

[]:             Quote Post by Oz » Fri Dec 19, 2014 5:35 am Some films and shows are naturally not suited for subtitles, as you mentioned, but it is also up to the translator to skim the dialogue so that the essential remains and it's easy enough to read. And besides, fan subtitles for anime series tend to be of varying quality, often very fast-paced and full of direct loan words from Japanese, which makes them a sort of a special case anyhow. What I'm mostly irritated about is the complaints about the subtitles for anything not aimed at an otaku demographic (and preferably the work of actual hired translators, not free fan subs found online). As for the ability to process the subtitles, in Finland (and pretty much everywhere else in the world, I assume) AV translators are told to time their subtitles so that even grannies in (and past) their 80's can read them easily. So that only applies to a very specific and restricted area of subtitled media.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Dec 19, 2014 7:52 am I can't blame anyone for hating subs if their experience with them has come from Funimation Blu-Rays. So many white subtitles with thin black outlines, so many of them unreadable without painfully straining one's eyes. Hey, fellas, legible subtitles have existed for a long time -- this really isn't that difficult!

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Fri Dec 19, 2014 8:22 am You can say anything you want, nothing will never goes even close to the French 80's/90's dubs in terms of so bad it's hilarious, NOTHING!

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Fri Dec 19, 2014 12:00 pm My sister has an odd habit of turing on the English subtitles of an American-made film. I never understood that myself, as both the subs and the language track is in English. Maybe she just wanted to experience the film in as thorough as way as it could be presented to her. Either that or she just got used to watching movies with our grandfather, who couldn't hear all that well. If I’ve seen the foreign film in question recently, I’ll sometimes watch it again in its original language without the English subs. It gives me a chance to enjoy both the original performances and an uncluttered screen.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Dec 19, 2014 1:50 pm Reichu wrote: Blu-Rays. So many white subtitles with thin black outlines

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Dec 20, 2014 12:34 am FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: My sister has an odd habit of turing on the English subtitles of an American-made film. I never understood that myself

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Sat Dec 20, 2014 1:19 am I'm the same on all counts there, Reichu. I always watch with subtitles whenever I have them, and there are a lot of times I've missed or misheard dialogue. I'm playing Dead Space right now and I had to play back the logs I picked up more than once until I thought to turn on the subtitles (which I thought I'd set only to realize they were reset when I started the game back over to up the difficulty). In some cases I wound up completely misreading the situation going on in a log because some critical word happened to occur at the same time as a sharp background noise (usually in the game environment rather than as part of the log). And I prefer yellow DVD subtitles by far. White with thin outline gets lost way too easily in the coloration of some scenes. (It's a problem I've been having in Assassin's Creed 3 as well.) If they were going to do white they should have done it with thick black outlines, Lucasfilm adventure game style.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Sat Dec 20, 2014 2:57 am My wife is part deaf, and watches English films and TV with subtitles. But some of the BDs I've got don't let you turn the subtitles on unless you're watching in the original language; who would make such a decision?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Sat Dec 20, 2014 9:41 pm pwhodges wrote: But some of the BDs I've got don't let you turn the subtitles on unless you're watching in the original language; who would make such a decision?
